case_studies.title: Preventive Cardiology in a Child with Congenital Heart Disease
case_studies.vignette: A 9-month-old boy with unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot presents to the pediatric outpatient clinic during the winter season for worsening irritability and reduced feeding over the past two days. His mother reports that he has been more lethargic than usual and has had a low-grade fever for the last 24 hours. The family lives in a semi-urban area, and his father recently had a cold but did not seek medical attention. On examination, the child appears pale and mildly cyanotic with a resting oxygen saturation of 82%. His respiratory rate is 50 breaths per minute with mild subcostal retractions, and auscultation reveals clear lung fields with no added sounds. His capillary refill time is 3 seconds, and the mother mentions he has had fewer wet diapers in the last 24 hours. The family is unsure about their recent vaccination status, including influenza immunization, and reports inconsistent hand hygiene practices at home.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the importance of influenza and pneumococcal vaccination, as well as household cocooning, in preventing severe respiratory infections and decompensation in children with congenital heart disease.

case_studies.title: Preventive Cardiology: Immunization and Household Cocooning in CHD
case_studies.vignette: A 6-month-old male infant with unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot presents to a rural health center in India with a 2-day history of poor feeding, irritability, and low-grade fever. His mother reports that his older sibling recently had a 'cold' with cough and runny nose. The family lives in a crowded household with limited ventilation. On examination, the infant is afebrile but tachypneic (RR 58/min) with mild subcostal retractions. Oxygen saturation is 82% on room air, consistent with his baseline. His weight is below the 3rd percentile for age. Auscultation reveals a loud systolic murmur at the left upper sternal border. The mother admits they were unaware of the need for influenza vaccination for the family and reveals that the infant has not received the pneumococcal conjugate vaccine due to financial constraints. The health worker notes that the infant's condition has worsened since his last cardiology visit, with increasing cyanosis and fatigue during feeds.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the importance of influenza and pneumococcal vaccination, as well as household cocooning, in preventing severe respiratory infections in children with congenital heart disease, particularly those with unrepaired lesions or cyanosis.

case_studies.title: Preventive Cardiology: Immunization and Cocooning in a Child with CHD
case_studies.vignette: A 6-month-old male infant with unrepaired ventricular septal defect (VSD) presents to a rural health center with a 2-day history of low-grade fever and poor feeding. His mother reports that he has been breathing faster than usual and seems more irritable. The family lives in a crowded household with limited access to clean water, and his 4-year-old sibling recently had a 'cold and cough.' On examination, the infant has a respiratory rate of 58 breaths/min, mild intercostal retractions, and oxygen saturation of 92% on room air. Auscultation reveals a loud pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border and bilateral crackles at the lung bases. The infant appears mildly dehydrated with a capillary refill time of 3 seconds. His immunization card shows he has not received the influenza vaccine, and his pneumococcal vaccination is incomplete. The mother is unaware of the need for household members to be immunized to protect the child. A decision must be made regarding the need for admission and further evaluation.
case_studies.learning_objective: Highlight the importance of influenza and pneumococcal immunization, as well as household cocooning, in preventing severe respiratory infections in children with congenital heart disease.

subtopics.subtopic_title: LESION-SPECIFIC LONGITUDINAL FOLLOW-UP AND SELECTIVE ENDOCARDITIS PROPHYLAXIS IN CONGENITAL HEART DI
subtopics.category: INFECTION PREVENTION LONGITUDINAL SURVEILLANCE AND PROCEDURAL PROPHYLAXIS IN CONGENITAL HEART DISEAS
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Preventive care in congenital heart disease (CHD) requires early recognition and lesion-specific longitudinal planning. Newborns presenting with cyanosis, weak or differential femoral pulses, unexplained shock, persistent tachypnea, poor feeding, or a pathologic murmur should be urgently evaluated for CHD, including duct-dependent lesions. Follow-up after diagnosis or repair must address not just anatomy but also growth, nutrition, anemia, exercise tolerance, pulmonary hypertension risk, and developmental participation.
- **Clinical application:** Mild lesions such as small ventricular septal defects or mild aortic stenosis typically require annual or biennial cardiology reviews with echocardiography every 1 to 2 years. Moderate-complexity lesions, whether repaired or unrepaired, usually necessitate follow-up every 6 to 12 months. In cases of unrepaired cyanotic physiology or single-ventricle circulation, closer specialist surveillance is often essential. Infective endocarditis prophylaxis is reserved for high-risk conditions, including prosthetic valves, prior infective endocarditis, unrepaired cyanotic CHD, repaired CHD with prosthetic material within 6 months, or residual defects adjacent to prosthetic patches or devices.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Routine antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended for simple lesions, aligning with current AHA guidance. Families of patients qualifying for infective endocarditis prophylaxis should receive clear written instructions or a prophylaxis card to ensure adherence. Surveillance must be individualized based on lesion complexity and repair status, with a focus on preventing complications such as pulmonary hypertension and ensuring optimal growth and development.

subtopics.subtopic_title: COMMUNITY COUNSELING POLICY ADVOCACY AND FOLLOW-UP SYSTEMS FOR SUSTAINED RISK REDUCTION
subtopics.category: SCHOOL AND COMMUNITY IMPLEMENTATION OF PEDIATRIC PREVENTIVE CARDIOLOGY IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** Sustained cardiovascular risk reduction in children requires community-based implementation rather than relying solely on clinic-based advice. Pediatricians should advocate for systemic changes such as healthier school meals with reduced salt and ultra-processed foods, regular physical education, safe walking or cycling options, and restrictions on tobacco exposure and marketing of sugar-sweetened beverages. These measures aim to create environments where healthy choices are easier and more accessible for children.
- **Clinical application:** Counseling should be integrated into routine pediatric interactions, including immunization visits, adolescent clinics, anganwadi or school platforms, and chronic disease follow-up visits, ensuring consistent reinforcement of prevention messages. Practical recommendations include promoting water instead of sweetened drinks, daily active play, adequate sleep, continued breastfeeding into the second year, early treatment of sore throat or impetigo, and adherence to benzathine penicillin prophylaxis or cardiology reviews. Public communication should remain non-stigmatizing and culturally sensitive to maximize community engagement and adherence.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Follow-up systems are critical for sustained outcomes and can include registries, reminder calls, text messaging, school coordination, and community health worker involvement. These systems enhance continuity of care for conditions like obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and rheumatic fever prophylaxis. Pediatric societies and clinicians should also engage in policy advocacy for healthy-school standards, food labeling, and stronger tobacco-control measures, ensuring that preventive cardiology efforts are embedded within broader community systems.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SCHOOL SCREENING PATHWAYS AND LINKAGE TO RASHTRIYA BAL SWASTHYA KARYAKRAM SERVICES
subtopics.category: SCHOOL AND COMMUNITY IMPLEMENTATION OF PEDIATRIC PREVENTIVE CARDIOLOGY IN INDIA
subtopics.concept:
- **Core concept:** School and public screening programs can significantly enhance pediatric preventive cardiology in India by identifying risk factors like raised body mass index, abnormal blood pressure, tobacco exposure, exertional symptoms, or suspicious murmurs early. The Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) provides a structured framework for screening children from birth to 18 years for defects at birth, diseases, deficiencies, and developmental delays, with referral pathways for higher-level care. Linking positive findings from school screenings to actionable follow-up is essential to prevent loss to follow-up and ensure continuity of care.
- **Clinical application:** A practical school screening pathway includes repeat measurement of abnormal blood pressure, referral of persistent abnormalities, counseling for overweight and inactivity, and echocardiography referral for murmurs or symptoms suggestive of rheumatic or congenital heart disease. Waist circumference should be recorded in overweight or obese children, and a simple repeat-review log should be maintained to improve follow-up. Pediatricians should align clinic documentation with RBSK to ensure seamless transitions from school or community screenings to clinical care. Using consistent referral thresholds across school and clinic settings reduces confusion and supports better uptake of follow-up assessments.
- **Safety/follow-up:** Children identified with obesity, hypertension, suspected rheumatic heart disease, or exercise-related red flags require a named follow-up site, family education in simple language, and a defined reassessment interval. Without these measures, screening efforts risk becoming mere paperwork without meaningful prevention. Pediatricians should ensure that families understand the importance of follow-up and that referral pathways are clear, actionable, and time-bound to optimize outcomes.

case_studies.title: School Screening Identifies Elevated Blood Pressure in a 12-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy in a rural government school is screened during a routine health camp under the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK). He has no complaints of chest pain, palpitations, or breathlessness but mentions feeling 'tired' during physical activity in recent months. His BMI is at the 95th percentile for his age, and his waist circumference is elevated. Blood pressure measured during the screening is 138/90 mmHg, which is repeated after 10 minutes of rest and found to be 136/88 mmHg. The school health team notes a loud systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border during auscultation. The boy’s parents are informed and express concern but are unsure how to proceed due to financial constraints and limited access to specialist care in their area.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the role of school screening in identifying risk factors for pediatric cardiovascular disease and the importance of linking findings to RBSK services for follow-up and continuity of care.

case_studies.title: School Screening Uncovers Hypertension in a 12-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy studying in Class 7 at a government school in rural India was screened during a routine health camp organized under the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK). The school health team noted his body mass index (BMI) was at the 95th percentile for his age and gender. His blood pressure (BP), measured twice with an appropriately sized cuff, was 132/88 mmHg, which was above the 95th percentile for his age, height, and gender. The child denied any symptoms like chest pain, dizziness, or fatigue during exertion. However, his waist circumference was also noted to be above the 90th percentile. On examination, no murmurs or other abnormalities were detected. The parents were informed, but they expressed concerns about traveling to the nearest district hospital due to financial constraints and lack of awareness. The screening team emphasized the importance of follow-up and provided a referral slip for further evaluation. A follow-up visit to the school was scheduled in two weeks to review the family's progress.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the importance of linking school screening findings, such as elevated BMI and blood pressure, to actionable follow-up measures under the RBSK framework to ensure continuity of care and prevent loss to follow-up.

case_studies.title: School Screening Identifies a Child with Hypertension and Obesity
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy from a government school in rural India is screened during a health camp under the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK). He reports no current symptoms but is noted to have difficulty keeping up during physical education classes. His body mass index (BMI) is above the 95th percentile for his age, and his waist circumference is also elevated. His blood pressure, measured twice during the screening, is 130/85 mmHg, both readings exceeding the age- and height-adjusted 95th percentile. A focused physical exam reveals no murmurs or other findings suggestive of structural heart disease. The school health team refers him to the nearest district hospital for further evaluation and counseling. His parents are informed about the findings and the need for follow-up but express concerns about the time and cost of additional visits. The screening team documents the findings in the RBSK logbook and provides a referral slip with instructions for the family.
case_studies.learning_objective: To understand the role of school-based screening in identifying pediatric cardiovascular risk factors and ensuring linkage to care through structured referral pathways like those provided by RBSK.

case_studies.title: School Screening Identifies Hypertension in a 10-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 10-year-old boy from a rural government school in India is evaluated during an annual school health screening under the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK). The initial screening reveals a blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg, measured with an appropriately sized cuff. The child is asymptomatic but has a history of sedentary behavior and frequent consumption of packaged snacks. His BMI is at the 95th percentile for age and gender. Waist circumference is also above the recommended threshold for his age. A repeat blood pressure measurement after 1 week confirms persistence of elevated readings (138/88 mmHg). No other symptoms such as chest pain, dyspnea, or exertional fatigue are reported. Focused examination reveals no murmurs or signs of heart failure, and peripheral pulses are normal. The school health team advises the family to follow up at the nearest district hospital but notes that the parents express limited understanding of the potential implications of the findings.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the role of school screening programs like RBSK in identifying early cardiovascular risk factors and the importance of linking abnormal findings to structured follow-up pathways to prevent loss to care.
